title: Don't report a failed refresh after the summary was already created.
prompt:
```
Verify each finding against current code. Fix only still-valid issues, skip the
rest with a brief reason, keep changes minimal, and validate.

In `@apps/web/src/app/api/v1/ops/space-memory/refresh-discussions/route.ts` around
lines 145 - 159, The current flow treats enqueueSignalEvaluationFromMemory as
part of the primary operation and can flip a successful summary
(result.summaryId pushed into summaries) into a failure if the enqueue throws;
wrap the call to enqueueSignalEvaluationFromMemory in its own try/catch, log or
process the enqueue error (using a logger or processLogger) inside the catch,
and ensure you still push the successful summary entry to summaries when
result.ok is true so retries won't recreate the saved summary; reference
enqueueSignalEvaluationFromMemory, the summaries array push, and
result.ok/result.summaryId when applying this change.
```
body:
_⚠️ Potential issue_ | _🟠 Major_ | _⚡ Quick win_

**Don't report a failed refresh after the summary was already created.**

Lines 145-158 treat `enqueueSignalEvaluationFromMemory` as part of the primary operation. If summary creation succeeds and only the follow-up enqueue throws, this worker records the space as failed even though the summary is already persisted. Catch and log the enqueue error separately so retries do not create duplicate discussion summaries.

<details>
<summary>💡 Minimal fix</summary>

```diff
         if (result.ok) {
-          await enqueueSignalEvaluationFromMemory(
-            {
-              spaceSlug,
-              triggerKind: 'ops_refresh',
-            },
-            { db },
-          );
+          try {
+            await enqueueSignalEvaluationFromMemory(
+              {
+                spaceSlug,
+                triggerKind: 'ops_refresh',
+              },
+              { db },
+            );
+          } catch (error) {
+            console.error(
+              '[space-memory.refresh-discussions] Failed to enqueue signal evaluation',
+              { spaceSlug, error },
+            );
+          }
           summaries.push({
             space_slug: spaceSlug,
             ok: true,
```
</details>


As per coding guidelines: `apps/web/**` route handlers and middleware handle errors gracefully.

<summary>🤖 Prompt for AI Agents</summary>

title: Restrict URL fields to `http`/`https` only.
prompt:
```

In `@packages/chat-server/src/tools/create-space-from-onboarding.ts` around lines
```

```diff
 });
```

body:
_⚠️ Potential issue_ | _🟠 Major_ | _⚡ Quick win_

**Make confirmation parsing strict (not broad substring checks).**

Line 35 accepts any text that merely contains `confirm` plus token, which is overly permissive for approval-sensitive operations. Require a stricter pattern (e.g., exact token/command match with word boundaries).  
 

<details>
<summary>Suggested change</summary>

```diff
 export function hasExplicitConfirmation(
   lastUserText: string | null | undefined,
   token: string,
 ): boolean {
   if (!lastUserText) return false;
-  const normalized = lastUserText.toLowerCase();
-  return (
-    normalized.includes('confirm') && normalized.includes(token.toLowerCase())

+  const pattern = new RegExp(`\\bconfirm\\b\\s+${escapedToken}\\b`);
+  return pattern.test(normalized);
 }
```

Repository: hypha-dao/hypha-web

---

🌐 Web query:

`Web Speech API SpeechRecognition.stop() asynchronous final result behavior`

💡 Result:

In the Web Speech API, the SpeechRecognition.stop method is designed to stop the speech recognition service from listening to incoming audio and, crucially, it attempts to return a SpeechRecognitionResult based on the audio captured up to that point [1][2][3]. This behavior is asynchronous [1][2]. When you call stop, the service does not immediately return a result; instead, it processes the audio already received and, if a valid recognition can be formed, it will fire a result event [4][5]. If the service successfully processes the captured audio, the result event will be triggered, providing the final transcript [4][6]. Following the result event, the end event is typically fired to indicate that the service has disconnected [1][7]. Because this process is asynchronous, you should not expect the transcript to be available immediately on the line following the stop call [2][3]. Instead, you must listen for the result event to handle the final transcript once it is returned by the service [4][5]. If no significant recognition can be made from the captured audio, the service may fire a nomatch event instead [1][7].

Citations:

- 1: https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/API/SpeechRecognition
- 2: https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/SpeechRecognition/stop
- 3: https://github.com/mdn/content/blob/main/files/en-us/web/api/speechrecognition/stop/index.md?plain=1
- 4: https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/SpeechRecognition/result_event
- 5: https://tirnocorn.info/?_=%2Fen-US%2Fdocs%2FWeb%2FAPI%2FWeb_Speech_API%2FUsing_the_Web_Speech_API%23uKrPXpWVbUDOQlJJrn8IgaLw1kGcxi4DywlLXqU%3D
- 6: https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/SpeechRecognitionEvent/results
- 7: https://devdoc.net/web/developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/SpeechRecognition.html

---

🌐 Web query:

`Web Speech API SpeechRecognition handle final result event after stop() best practice`

💡 Result:

When using the Web Speech API's SpeechRecognition interface, the stop method is designed to stop the service from listening to new audio while attempting to return any results captured up to that point [1][2][3]. Because the API is asynchronous and event-driven, handling the final result after calling stop requires relying on the result event rather than a return value from the stop method itself [1][4]. Best Practices for Handling Final Results: 1. Rely on the result event: The SpeechRecognition service will continue to fire result events if it has pending data to process after stop is called [1][3]. You should listen for the result event and check the isFinal property of the SpeechRecognitionResult [1][5][6]. 2. Avoid assuming immediate completion: Calling stop does not guarantee that a result is returned instantly [2]. The browser may still be processing the final audio buffer. Your application logic should remain in a state ready to receive the final result event [4]. 3. Use the end event for cleanup: The end event is the reliable signal that the recognition service has fully disconnected [3]. This is the appropriate place to perform UI cleanup, re-enable buttons, or reset state, as it fires regardless of whether the session ended via stop, abort, or an automatic timeout [7][8][9]. 4. Distinguish stop from abort: Use stop when you want to process the audio captured so far [2][3]. Use abort if you want to immediately stop the service and discard any pending results without attempting to return them [10][3]. Example Pattern: recognition.addEventListener('result', (event) => { for (const result of event.results) { if (result.isFinal) { console.log('Final transcript:', result.transcript); // Process your final result here } } }); recognition.addEventListener('end', => { console.log('Recognition service disconnected.'); // Perform cleanup or restart logic here }); // Triggering the stop recognition.stop; Note that the Web Speech API's behavior can vary significantly between browsers, and some implementations may automatically stop listening after periods of silence [7][9]. If you require continuous listening, you may need to implement a strategy to restart the recognition in the end event handler, while using a flag to distinguish between intentional stops and automatic timeouts [7][8].

Citations:

- 1: https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/SpeechRecognition
- 2: https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/SpeechRecognition/stop
- 3: https://devdoc.net/web/developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/SpeechRecognition.html
- 4: https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/SpeechRecognition/result_event
- 5: https://webreflection.medium.com/taming-the-web-speech-api-ef64f5a245e1
- 6: https://wiki.developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/SpeechRecognitionResult
- 7: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/41324307/web-speech-api-stops-listening-after-some-time-passes-without-input
- 8: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/42895760/how-to-stop-and-restart-web-speech-api-correctly
- 9: https://medium.com/@johnteckert/say-that-again-ed8667b689ca
- 10: https://devdoc.net/web/developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/SpeechRecognition/abort.html

---

🌐 Web query:

`Web Speech API SpeechRecognition stop() await final result properly`

💡 Result:

The Web Speech API's SpeechRecognition.stop method is not an asynchronous operation that returns a promise, so it cannot be directly awaited [1]. Instead, it stops the service from listening to new audio and attempts to return any results captured up to that point [2][1]. To properly handle the final result when stopping, you must rely on the event-driven nature of the API [2][3]. Key Implementation Strategies: 1. Use the result event: The SpeechRecognition service communicates results asynchronously via the result event [3]. You should listen for this event and check the isFinal property of the result to determine if the transcription is complete [4][5]. 2. Wrap in a Promise: Since the API is callback-based, you can wrap the recognition process in a Promise to make it compatible with async/await syntax [6]. This allows you to resolve the promise only when a final result is received [6]. Example Pattern: function getSpeechResult { return new Promise((resolve, reject) => { const recognition = new (window.SpeechRecognition || window.webkitSpeechRecognition); recognition.onresult = (event) => { const result = event.results[event.resultIndex]; if (result.isFinal) { resolve(result.transcript); recognition.stop; } }; recognition.onerror = (event) => reject(event.error); recognition.start; }); } // Usage try { const transcript = await getSpeechResult; console.log("Final result:", transcript); } catch (error) { console.error("Recognition error:", error); } Important Distinctions: - stop vs. abort: The stop method attempts to return results captured so far [1]. In contrast, the abort method stops the service immediately without attempting to return any pending results [7]. - Event Handling: Because the API is inherently asynchronous and event-based, simply calling stop does not guarantee that a final result event will fire immediately or at all if no audio has been processed [2][1]. Always ensure your logic handles the result event independently of the stop call [3].

Citations:

- 1: https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/SpeechRecognition/stop
- 2: https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/SpeechRecognition
- 3: https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/SpeechRecognition/result_event
- 4: https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/SpeechRecognitionResult/isFinal
- 5: https://www.assemblyai.com/blog/speech-recognition-javascript-web-speech-api
- 6: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/57004583/how-to-wait-for-speechsynthesis-to-end-before-starting-speechrecognition-and-vic
- 7: https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/SpeechRecognition/abort

---



</details>

**Wait for transcription shutdown before returning the final transcript.**

`recognition.stop()` is asynchronous and does not block. The `stop()` method returns the transcript immediately via `chunks.join(' ').trim()`, but the Web Speech API may still emit a final `result` event after `stop()` is called. Any chunks delivered in that final event are lost because the function has already returned. To fix this, wrap the recognition session in a Promise that resolves only when a `result` event with `isFinal: true` is received, then resolve the stop call accordingly.
